prototype). 43) Quarantine: “forty days” → any isolation period (bleaching of exact number). 44) Candidate: “clothed in white” → person running for office (metonymy from ritual to role). 45) Rival: “one sharing a stream” (Latin rivalis) → competitor (social extension). 46) Sponsor: “guarantor” → supporter/funder (role broadening). 47) Patron: “protector” → customer/supporter (shift in social institutions). 48) Clinic: “bed” (Greek kline) → outpatient medical facility (metonymy from setting to institution). 49) Fetish: “charm/object” → object of irrational devotion or sexual fixation (broadening + specialization). 50) Virus: biological agent → malware → viral popularity (metaphor + broadening).
That list is gold. Now I want to zoom in on “fond” because you slipped it in—“fond” used to mean foolish?

Yes. “Fond” is a neat amelioration case. Earlier sense - In Middle English and Early Modern English, fond commonly meant “foolish, silly, stupid,” often with the sense “lacking good judgment.” You can still catch echoes in older literary contexts where “fond” means “foolish” (“fond hope” sometimes retains a trace: a hope that is foolish or unrealistic). Pathway to modern sense 1) “Foolish” → “foolishly affectionate” A person can be “fond” of someone in a way that implies doting, perhaps excessive or unwise affection. That’s a pragmatic bridge: the “foolish” evaluation is attached to the intensity of affection. 2) Loss of negative evaluation As “fond of” becomes a common collocation meaning “having affection for,” speakers stop hearing it as “foolishly” and hear it as simply “affectionately.” This is bleaching of the “foolish” component in that collocation. 3) Dominance of the affectionate sense Over time, the affectionate sense becomes default; the “foolish” sense becomes archaic, surviving in fixed or literary phrases (and sometimes in “fondly” in older texts meaning “foolishly”). Why one won - “Fond of” is a high-frequency phrase in everyday life; high-frequency frames are powerful engines for semantic change. - Social utility: English needs a mild adjective for affection; “fond” fits well and gets entrenched. So modern fond is a “rescued” word: a former insult domesticated into a gentle emotion term via a common syntactic frame.
Could you do a comparative mini-essay: why do some words pejorate (villain, hussy) while others ameliorate (nice, fond)? Is there a general principle?

There are tendencies rather than laws, but several robust pressures show up across languages: 1) Social hierarchy pressure (pejoration) Words for lower-status groups often pejorate because elites’ attitudes get encoded in everyday descriptors. - villain (peasant → bad person) - churl (peasant/free man → rude person) - boor (farmer → crude person) This is structurally common: labels for out-groups accumulate contempt. 2) Taboo and moral policing (pejoration, especially sexual) Terms touching sex, bodily functions, and stigmatized behavior often become insults or “dirty words” because they’re used in condemnation. - hussy (housewife → improper woman) - lewd (lay/unlearned → sexual indecency) - vulgar itself: “of the common people” → “crude/obscene” 3) Euphemism treadmill (often pejoration over time) A polite term for a stigmatized thing gets used more and more for that stigmatized thing, so it inherits the stigma, and a new euphemism replaces it. This is common in disability terms, poverty terms, and bodily functions. 4) Politeness and affection niches (amelioration) Words can ameliorate when they become embedded in polite formulas or affectionate collocations. - fond of (affection collocation bleaches negative) - nice moves through “precise/fastidious” to “pleasant” partly because it becomes a general-purpose social approval adjective. 5) Generalization of praise terms (bleaching into mild positive) Strong positive words often bleach into mild positives because speakers use them as default compliments. - terrific, awesome (intensity → general approval) This is less “amelioration” than “bleaching + pragmatic strengthening,” but the outcome is a positive drift. 6) The “semantic gravity” of frequent contexts A word’s most frequent use can drag its meaning. - If “hussy” starts appearing mostly in scolding contexts, it slides negative. - If “nice” appears in polite approval contexts, it slides positive. So the best general principle is: meaning follows usage, and usage follows social evaluation. Words pejorate when they’re used to mark outsiders, stigma, or condemnation; they ameliorate when they’re used in politeness, affection, or generalized approval—especially in high-frequency frames.

This is exactly the kind of nerding-out I wanted. One more: can you give me a “semantic drift map” for a single root across multiple descendants—like Latin “caput” or something—showing divergent paths?

Take Latin caput “head” as a root that spawns a whole semantic constellation. You get both direct borrowings and learned compounds, and they diverge into anatomy, leadership, counting, and even crime/punishment. Root: caput “head” Core conceptual cluster: head = top, source, leader, unit, life. Descendants and drift paths: 1) Capital - From capitalis “of the head.” - Path A (punishment): “capital punishment” = punishment affecting the head/life → death penalty (metonymy: head/life). - Path B (importance): “capital city” = head city → principal city (metaphor: head as leader). - Path C (money): capital = principal wealth/stock (metaphor: the “head” or principal sum; accounting sense). These senses coexist but are contextually partitioned. 2) Captain - From capitaneus “chief, head.” - Drift: “head person” → military/naval leader → any team leader. Still transparently “head” metaphor. 3) Chief - From Old French chef, ultimately from caput. - Drift: head → leader → most important. “Chef” in French also means “chief,” but in English “chef” specialized to head cook (occupational narrowing in borrowing). 4) Chef (as borrowed into English) - Borrowed relatively late with a specialized culinary meaning. - Drift: “chief” in French → “head cook” in English (narrowing by domain). 5) Cap / caput in learned terms - Decapitate: remove the head → behead (literal). - Capitulate: from capitulum “little head/chapter,” i.e., headings of an agreement → surrender on terms (metonymy: “chapters/heads” of conditions → accept terms). - Chapter: from capitulum (a heading) → a division of a book (metonymy from headings to sections). 6) Capitalize - In writing: make a letter “capital” (head-sized/major letter). - In finance: convert into capital; exploit for gain (metaphor from wealth stock). 7) Cattle / chattel (more distant but related through “property counted by heads” in some analyses; etymological paths are complex and not always directly from caput in English, so I’ll flag it as conceptually linked rather than a clean descendant set). What this shows - One concrete body-part concept becomes a generative metaphor source: leadership (“head of state”), primacy (“chief”), main sum (“capital”), and life itself (“capital punishment”). - Divergence happens because different borrowing routes (French vs learned Latin), different institutions (law, government, cuisine), and different metaphors get entrenched. If you want an even cleaner “family tree,” Latin manus “hand” is also great: manual, manufacture, maneuver, manicure, manuscript—lots of drift driven by “hand” as agency and skill.
I keep mixing up indicative and subjunctive in Spanish. Is there a simple rule for when to use the subjunctive? I’m losing my mind.

The simplest rule that actually works: use the subjunctive when the clause is not presented as a fact, but as something subjective, uncertain, desired, feared, denied, or not yet real—especially after “que.” A practical way to decide fast is the “trigger + que + different subject” pattern. If you have: 1) a main clause with a trigger (want, hope, doubt, deny, emotion, necessity, recommendation, possibility), 2) “que,” and 3) a different subject in the second clause, then the second clause is usually subjunctive. Examples: - Quiero que vengas. (I want you to come.) - Dudo que sea verdad. (I doubt it’s true.) - Es importante que estudies. (It’s important that you study.) - Me alegra que estés aquí. (I’m glad you’re here.) If it’s presented as an objective statement of reality, you’re generally in indicative: - Sé que vienes. (I know you’re coming.) - Es verdad que estudias. (It’s true that you study.) We can make this much more reliable if you tell me which structures you’re mixing up (e.g., “cuando,” “aunque,” “para que,” “creo que,” “es posible que,” etc.).
Okay but that “not a fact” thing always feels fuzzy. Like, “I think he’s coming” is not a fact either, right? Yet sometimes it’s indicative, sometimes subjunctive. I hate this.

You’re right to hate the “fact vs not fact” slogan because it’s oversimplified. A better distinction is: indicative = speaker commits to the proposition as true (or at least treats it as true in the conversation). Subjunctive = speaker does not commit to it, or frames it as desired/uncertain/valued rather than asserted. With “creer/pensar” you get a clean contrast: Indicative (affirmative belief = commitment): - Creo que viene. (I believe he’s coming.) → You’re presenting it as your belief, which functions as an assertion. Subjunctive (negated belief = lack of commitment): - No creo que venga. (I don’t think he’s coming.) → You’re explicitly withholding belief. Also subjunctive in questions where you’re not committing: - ¿Crees que venga? (Do you think he’s coming?) In many regions this can be subjunctive because you’re not asserting; you’re asking about the likelihood. But note: in practice, many speakers use indicative here too: ¿Crees que viene? Both exist; nuance and regional preference matter. So the rule isn’t “fact,” it’s “assertion vs non-assertion.” Affirmative “creo que” asserts; negative “no creo que” does not.
But then why is “No dudo que…” indicative? I saw “No dudo que es verdad” somewhere and it broke my brain. If “no creo que” is subjunctive, shouldn’t “no dudo que” be subjunctive too?

Great example because it shows why you can’t just map “negation = subjunctive.” “Dudar que” is a doubt trigger: - Dudo que sea verdad. (I doubt it’s true.) → subjunctive. But “no dudar que” flips the meaning to certainty. “I don’t doubt that…” is basically “I’m sure that…,” which is an assertion, so indicative is common: - No dudo que es verdad. (I don’t doubt it’s true.) → indicative (asserted). That said, you will also see subjunctive after “no dudo que” in real Spanish: - No dudo que sea verdad. This can happen because the verb “dudar” is strongly associated with subjunctive, and some speakers keep subjunctive even under negation, or they use it to sound more formal/hedged. But the “logic” pushes toward indicative because the speaker is expressing confidence. So: - Dudo que + subj. - No dudo que + often indicative, sometimes subj (variation/nuance). If you want a safe, exam-style guideline: “dudar que” → subjunctive; “no dudar que” → indicative (especially in neutral written Spanish). If you want real-life nuance: both occur; indicative emphasizes certainty.
This is exactly why I’m frustrated. It feels like there’s always an exception or “depends.” Can you give me something more mechanical? Like a checklist?

Yes—use a checklist by construction type. Spanish subjunctive is less “one rule” and more “sets of environments.” Here’s a mechanical decision tree: A) Is it a relative clause (who/that/which) describing something that may or may not exist? - If the antecedent is unknown/nonexistent/desired: subjunctive. - Busco un libro que sea fácil. (I’m looking for a book that’s easy.) → not a specific known book. - If the antecedent is known/specific: indicative. - Busco el libro que es fácil. (I’m looking for the easy book.) → that specific one. B) Is it a time clause with “cuando/en cuanto/hasta que/antes de que/después de que” referring to the future? - Future/not yet happened: subjunctive. - Te llamaré cuando llegue. (I’ll call you when I arrive.) - Habitual/past: indicative. - Te llamo cuando llego. (I call you when I arrive [habitually].) - Te llamé cuando llegué. (I called you when I arrived.) C) Is it a “purpose” clause with “para que/a fin de que”? - Always subjunctive (because it’s about intention): - Lo hice para que me entendieras. D) Is it a noun clause after “que” triggered by: - Will/Influence: querer, pedir, recomendar, insistir en, dejar que… - Emotion: alegrarse de, temer, sorprender, molestar… - Doubt/Denial: dudar, negar, no creer, no es cierto… - Impersonal expressions: es importante, es necesario, es posible, es mejor… → Subjunctive. E) Is it a statement of certainty/knowledge/perception/reporting? - saber, es verdad, es evidente, está claro, creer (affirmative), pensar (affirmative), ver, oír… → Indicative. Then there are “connectors” like “aunque,” “como,” “mientras,” “tal vez,” which depend on meaning (certainty vs hypothetical). But the above A–E covers most cases mechanically. If you want, we can drill each category with minimal pairs until it becomes automatic.
Fine. Let’s do minimal pairs because I clearly need it. Start with “cuando.” I always mess up “cuando venga” vs “cuando viene.”

Good. “Cuando” is mostly about time reference: future vs habitual/past. 1) Future event (not happened yet) → subjunctive: - Cuando venga Juan, hablamos. (When Juan comes, we’ll talk.) The coming is pending. - Te lo diré cuando tenga tiempo. (I’ll tell you when I have time.) Not yet. 2) Habitual present (repeated, general truth) → indicative: - Cuando viene Juan, hablamos. (When Juan comes [as a habit], we talk.) - Cuando tengo tiempo, leo. (When I have time, I read.) 3) Past completed → indicative: - Cuando vino Juan, hablamos. (When Juan came, we talked.) - Te lo dije cuando tuve tiempo. (I told you when I had time.) A trick: if the main clause is in future or imperative, the “cuando” clause is very likely subjunctive: - Llámame cuando llegues. (Call me when you arrive.) - Me alegraré cuando lo sepas. (I’ll be happy when you know.) Now you: choose indicative or subjunctive and why. a) Te aviso cuando (salir) del trabajo. b) Te aviso cuando (salgo) del trabajo. (same verb, different meaning)
a) “Te aviso cuando salga del trabajo” because it’s in the future, I’ll let you know when I leave. b) “cuando salgo” is like “whenever I leave” or a routine? Is that right?

Exactly. a) Te aviso cuando salga del trabajo. → specific future departure (not yet). b) Te aviso cuando salgo del trabajo. → habitual: each time I leave work, I notify you (sounds like a routine arrangement). If you want to make b) sound more natural as habitual, you might add “siempre” or “normalmente,” but it’s grammatically fine. Now let’s add one that tends to trick people: - Cuando llegue, ya habrás salido. (When I arrive, you will already have left.) Both clauses are future-oriented; “cuando” clause is subjunctive.
Okay, “antes de que” is another one. Why is it always subjunctive? “Antes de que viene” sounds wrong but I don’t understand why.

“Antes de que” introduces an event that is not yet realized at the reference time; it’s inherently prospective (looking ahead), so Spanish treats it as non-asserted → subjunctive. - Salí antes de que viniera. (I left before he came.) At the moment of leaving, the coming hadn’t happened yet. Even though in reality he did come later, the clause is framed as “pending” relative to that time. Compare with “después de que,” which varies by dialect: - Después de que llegó/llegara… both exist. In many places, indicative is preferred when the event is viewed as factual (después de que llegó). Subjunctive can appear in more formal or narrative styles (después de que llegara), but it’s controversial prescriptively. But “antes de que” is strongly subjunctive across the board: - Antes de que llegue… - Antes de que llegara… - Antes de que hayas terminado… And note: if there is no subject change, you use infinitive instead: - Salí antes de venir. (I left before coming.) Same subject.
Wait, that last part—subject change. So “Antes de que yo venga” vs “Antes de venir.” That’s actually helpful. So subjunctive often appears when there’s a subject change and “que” is used?

Yes, that’s a very practical heuristic. If you can replace the subordinate clause with an infinitive, you usually don’t need subjunctive (because there’s no finite verb): - Espero verte. (same subject implicit) - Espero que me veas. (different subject) But careful: subject change isn’t the reason for subjunctive; it’s the reason you need a conjugated verb instead of an infinitive. The mood (subjunctive vs indicative) still depends on the type of clause/trigger. Still, the “que + different subject” pattern is a strong predictor for subjunctive in many common structures. Examples: - Quiero salir. (I want to leave.) → infinitive - Quiero que salgas. (I want you to leave.) → subjunctive - Es importante estudiar. (It’s important to study.) → infinitive - Es importante que estudies. → subjunctive
Okay. Now “aunque.” This one destroys me. Sometimes it’s subjunctive, sometimes indicative, and I can never tell.

“Aunque” depends on whether the speaker treats the information as known/real (indicative) or as hypothetical/irrelevant-to-truth (subjunctive). 1) Known fact / acknowledged reality → indicative: - Aunque está lloviendo, voy a salir. (Even though it’s raining, I’m going out.) You assert it’s raining. - Aunque me duele la cabeza, trabajo. (Even though my head hurts, I work.) It really hurts. 2) Hypothetical / unknown / “even if” → subjunctive: - Aunque llueva, voy a salir. (Even if it rains, I’m going out.) You’re not saying it’s raining; you’re saying it doesn’t matter whether it rains. - Aunque me duela la cabeza, voy a ir. (Even if my head hurts, I’ll go.) Not asserting the headache exists. A quick test: Can you translate it as “even if”? If yes, subjunctive is likely. If it’s clearly “even though” (concession about a known fact), indicative. Now you: choose mood. a) Aunque (ser) tarde, te espero. (You’re looking at the clock; it’s already late.) b) Aunque (ser) tarde, te esperaré. (You don’t know what time it’ll be; you’re saying you’ll wait regardless.)
a) “Aunque es tarde, te espero.” b) “Aunque sea tarde, te esperaré.” Right?
Exactly. In (a) it’s late right now—asserted. In (b) “even if it’s late”—hypothetical.
What about “tal vez” and “quizá(s)”? I’ve seen both moods after them and it feels random.

Not random; it reflects degree of certainty and stylistic choice. With “tal vez / quizá(s)”: - Indicative → speaker leans toward it being likely or is presenting it more neutrally as a possibility with some confidence. - Subjunctive → speaker presents it as more uncertain, speculative, or less committed. Examples: - Tal vez viene hoy. (Maybe he’s coming today.) Sounds like you think he probably is. - Tal vez venga hoy. (Maybe he’ll come today.) More uncertainty. - Quizás es verdad. (Perhaps it’s true.) Slightly more confident. - Quizás sea verdad. (Perhaps it’s true.) More tentative. In real usage, many speakers default to subjunctive with these adverbs, especially in careful speech, but indicative is very common. One more note: if you use future tense, you often keep indicative because the future already expresses uncertainty: - Quizás vendrá mañana. (Maybe he’ll come tomorrow.) Common. But you can also do: - Quizás venga mañana.

So Spanish can stack uncertainty markers. Great. That’s… wonderful. Another one: “es posible que” is always subjunctive, right? But why is “es cierto que” indicative? They look like the same structure.

Exactly: same structure, different meaning. - Es posible que + subjunctive because it expresses possibility, not assertion: - Es posible que esté enfermo. - Es cierto que / es verdad que + indicative because they assert truth: - Es cierto que está enfermo. - Es verdad que no vino. Similarly: - Es probable que + subj. - Es dudoso que + subj. - No es seguro que + subj. Versus: - Es obvio que + indic. - Es evidente que + indic. - Está claro que + indic. Some of those “obvio/evidente/claro” can take subjunctive in some varieties or in certain rhetorical uses (especially with negation or if the speaker is challenging the claim), but as a baseline: “certainty” → indicative; “possibility/necessity/value judgment” → subjunctive.
“Value judgment” like “es bueno que…”? That’s subjunctive too? But that’s basically stating an opinion, not doubt.

Right, and that’s a key point: subjunctive isn’t just about doubt. It’s also about evaluation and emotion. - Es bueno que estudies. (It’s good that you study.) You’re evaluating the action, not asserting it as a fact in the same way. (Though it might be true; the mood is chosen because the structure is evaluative.) Compare: - Es bueno estudiar. (It’s good to study.) general statement. - Es bueno que estudies. (It’s good that you study.) evaluation of your studying. Another evaluative set: - Me alegra que estés aquí. - Me molesta que hables así. - Es una lástima que no puedas venir. Even if the event is real (you are here), subjunctive appears because the main clause expresses emotion or judgment about it.
That one annoys me: “Me alegra que estés aquí.” You ARE here. Why not indicative? It feels like Spanish is denying reality.

It’s not denying reality; it’s marking the subordinate clause as “embedded under emotion,” i.e., not the main assertion. The sentence’s communicative point is “I’m glad,” not “you are here.” Spanish grammar treats the embedded proposition as backgrounded under the emotional reaction, so it goes subjunctive. English doesn’t mark this difference morphologically, so it feels weird. You can see the contrast if you switch the main verb: - Veo que estás aquí. (I see you’re here.) → indicative because it’s perception/knowledge. - Me alegra que estés aquí. → subjunctive because it’s emotion. Same reality, different framing.
Okay. Now commands. I know “quiero que + subjunctive,” but sometimes I see “que + subjunctive” alone as a command? Like “¡Que pase!” What is that?

That’s the “subjuntivo exhortativo” (jussive subjunctive): using “que + subjunctive” to express a command, wish, or instruction, often for third person or formal tone. Examples: - ¡Que pase el siguiente! (Let the next person come in.) - ¡Que lo haga él! (Let him do it!) - Que tengas un buen día. (Have a good day.) This is also a set wish. - Que se vayan. (Let them leave.) It’s common when you can’t use the imperative directly (especially for third person commands), or for softer/indirect commands.
Speaking of imperatives, why do “negative commands” use subjunctive? “No hables” etc. That feels arbitrary.

Historically and structurally, Spanish negative imperatives are formed with the present subjunctive. It’s not arbitrary in the modern system; it’s simply the rule: - Habla. (affirmative tú command uses imperative form) - No hables. (negative tú command uses present subjunctive) Same for usted/ustedes/nosotros, which already use present subjunctive even in affirmative commands: - Hable usted. - No hable usted. - Hablemos. - No hablemos. You can treat it as a paradigm fact to memorize: negative commands = present subjunctive.

Okay. Another pain point: “If” clauses. I know “si” doesn’t take subjunctive in present, but then “si tuviera” is subjunctive. And then “como si fuera.” Can you lay out the whole conditional thing cleanly?

Yes. Spanish conditionals have a fairly clean system: 1) Real/possible condition (present/future) → “si” + present indicative: - Si tengo tiempo, voy. (If I have time, I go / I’ll go.) - Si vienes mañana, te ayudo. (If you come tomorrow, I’ll help you.) 2) Hypothetical/unlikely present/future → “si” + imperfect subjunctive + conditional: - Si tuviera tiempo, iría. (If I had time, I would go.) - Si vinieras mañana, te ayudaría. (If you came tomorrow, I would help you.) 3) Counterfactual past → “si” + pluperfect subjunctive + conditional perfect: - Si hubiera tenido tiempo, habría ido. (If I had had time, I would have gone.) - Si hubieras venido, te habría ayudado. Now “como si” (as if) almost always takes imperfect subjunctive (or pluperfect subjunctive for past): - Habla como si supiera todo. (He talks as if he knew everything.) You imply it’s not true / it’s a comparison to an unreal scenario. - Me miró como si hubiera visto un fantasma. (He looked at me as if he had seen a ghost.) So: - “si” for real conditions uses indicative. - “si” for hypothetical/counterfactual uses subjunctive (imperfect/pluperfect). - “como si” uses subjunctive because it’s inherently unreal/contrary-to-fact.
That’s at least clean. But I still get stuck on imperfect subjunctive forms. “Hablara” vs “hablase.” Are they both real? Do people actually use “-ase”?

Both are real and correct: -ra and -se imperfect subjunctive forms. - hablara / hablase - tuviera / tuviese - fuera / fuese - pudiera / pudiese In most everyday speech across much of the Spanish-speaking world, -ra forms are more common. -se forms can sound more formal, literary, or simply regional preference. In many contexts they’re interchangeable. One nuance: in some literary styles, writers alternate -ra and -se to avoid repetition. Also, historically, -ra had indicative origins (from Latin pluperfect), but in modern Spanish it functions as subjunctive. For your purposes: pick -ra and stick with it unless your teacher/material strongly prefers -se.
Now present subjunctive formation. I know it’s “yo” present stem + opposite endings, but irregulars ruin everything. Can you summarize the irregulars that matter most?

Sure—present subjunctive has three main irregular patterns: 1) Irregular “yo” forms in present indicative carry into subjunctive stem: - tener → tengo → tenga, tengas, tenga, tengamos, tengáis, tengan - venir → vengo → venga… - poner → pongo → ponga… - salir → salgo → salga… - hacer → hago → haga… - decir → digo → diga… - traer → traigo → traiga… - oír → oigo → oiga… - conocer → conozco → conozca… - conducir → conduzco → conduzca… 2) Stem-changing verbs (e→ie, o→ue, e→i) apply in subjunctive, but with special patterns in nosotros/vosotros for -ar/-er vs -ir: - pensar: piense, pienses… pensemos, penséis (no stem change in nosotros/vosotros) - poder: pueda… podamos, podáis - pedir (-ir): pida, pidas… pidamos, pidáis (e→i persists in nosotros/vosotros for -ir) - dormir (-ir): duerma… durmamos, durmáis (o→u in nosotros/vosotros for -ir) - sentir (-ir): sienta… sintamos, sintáis (e→ie becomes e→i in nosotros/vosotros for -ir) 3) Spelling changes to preserve sound: - buscar → busque (c→qu before e) - pagar → pague (g→gu before e) - llegar → llegue (g→gu) - empezar → empiece (z→c before e) - tocar → toque (c→qu) - escoger → escoja (g→j before a) - proteger → proteja - construir → construya (y insertion) - leer → lea (i→y or vowel changes in some verbs) Plus a few highly irregular ones you just memorize: - ser → sea - ir → vaya - estar → esté - saber → sepa - dar → dé - haber → haya If you want, I can give you a compact “top 15” irregular subjunctives list to drill.
Give me that list. And also explain accent marks like “esté” and “dé.” I keep forgetting them.

Top high-frequency irregular present subjunctives (base forms): 1) ser → sea 2) ir → vaya 3) estar → esté (accent to keep stress: es-TÉ) 4) saber → sepa 5) dar → dé (accent distinguishes from preposition “de”) 6) haber → haya 7) tener → tenga 8) venir → venga 9) hacer → haga 10) decir → diga 11) poner → ponga 12) salir → salga 13) traer → traiga 14) oír → oiga 15) ver → vea (regular in pattern but spelling/pronunciation shift; common) Accent notes: - esté, estés, esté, estemos, estéis, estén: accent marks the stressed vowel because without it (“este”) it would be pronounced ES-te and also collides with “este” (this). - dé, des, dé, demos, deis, den: “dé” (verb “to give” in subjunctive/imperative usted) has an accent to distinguish it from “de” (of/from). Same with “sé” (I know / be!) vs “se” (reflexive pronoun), and “tú” vs “tu,” “mí” vs “mi,” etc.
Okay. Now can we do a big exercise? Like I’ll write sentences and you tell me if subjunctive is needed and fix them. Because I keep making wrong choices.

Yes. Write 10–15 sentences that include: “cuando,” “aunque,” “para que,” “quiero que,” “creo que/no creo que,” “es posible que/es cierto que,” a relative clause with “que,” and at least one “si” hypothetical. Make them about everyday neutral topics. I’ll correct mood, conjugation, and explain each.

Here goes. Don’t judge me. 1) Creo que él venga mañana. 2) No creo que él viene mañana. 3) Es posible que es tarde. 4) Es cierto que no haya clases hoy. 5) Quiero que tú vienes a tiempo. 6) Te llamaré cuando llego. 7) Cuando llego a casa, voy a dormir. (I meant future) 8) Aunque llueve, saldré. 9) Aunque llueva, salgo siempre. 10) Lo hago para que me entiendes. 11) Busco una persona que sabe mucho de gramática. 12) Si tendría más tiempo, aprendería más. 13) Me alegro que estás aquí. 14) Dudo que es verdad. 15) No dudo que sea verdad.

1) Creo que él venga mañana. → Usually indicative. Correct: Creo que (él) viene mañana. Reason: affirmative creer = assertion/belief. 2) No creo que él viene mañana. → Needs subjunctive. Correct: No creo que (él) venga mañana. Reason: negated belief = non-assertion. 3) Es posible que es tarde. → Needs subjunctive. Correct: Es posible que sea tarde. Reason: possibility trigger. 4) Es cierto que no haya clases hoy. → Usually indicative. Correct: Es cierto que no hay clases hoy. Reason: “es cierto que” asserts reality. 5) Quiero que tú vienes a tiempo. → Needs subjunctive. Correct: Quiero que (tú) vengas a tiempo. Reason: desire/influence. 6) Te llamaré cuando llego. → Future main clause → subjunctive. Correct: Te llamaré cuando llegue. 7) Cuando llego a casa, voy a dormir. (I meant future) → If future-specific, subjunctive. Correct: Cuando llegue a casa, voy a dormir. / Cuando llegue a casa, me voy a dormir. If habitual: Cuando llego a casa, voy a dormir. (each time) 8) Aunque llueve, saldré. → This is fine if it’s actually raining now. Meaning: Even though it’s raining (fact), I’ll go out. 9) Aunque llueva, salgo siempre. → Mixed message. If you mean “even if it rains, I always go out” (habit regardless of rain): Although it’s hypothetical each time, the main clause is habitual. This can work: Correct: Aunque llueva, salgo siempre. It’s actually okay: subjunctive expresses “regardless of whether,” while “salgo siempre” is habitual. If you meant “even though it rains (here a lot), I always go out,” then: Aunque llueve, salgo siempre. (indicative) 10) Lo hago para que me entiendes. → Purpose → subjunctive. Correct: Lo hago para que me entiendas. 11) Busco una persona que sabe mucho de gramática. → Depends: specific person or not? If you mean “I’m looking for someone (anyone) who knows…” (not identified): subjunctive. Correct: Busco una persona que sepa mucho de gramática. If you have someone in mind: Busco a una persona que sabe mucho… (indicative). 12) Si tendría más tiempo, aprendería más. → “si” hypothetical uses imperfect subjunctive. Correct: Si tuviera más tiempo, aprendería más. 13) Me alegro que estás aquí. → Emotion → subjunctive; also needs “de” often. Correct: Me alegro de que estés aquí. (Most standard) Some speakers drop “de,” but “me alegro de que” is safest. 14) Dudo que es verdad. → Doubt → subjunctive. Correct: Dudo que sea verdad. 15) No dudo que sea verdad. → As discussed, both moods appear. If you want the “logical/assertive” pattern: No dudo que es verdad. (indicative) But your version with subjunctive is also found and not shocking. If you’re aiming for consistent “assertion vs non-assertion,” use indicative here. If you want, we can rewrite 8–9 with very explicit meanings so you feel the difference.
Sentence 9 being “actually okay” makes me want to scream. How am I supposed to know when it’s okay? Also “Me alegro de que”—I never remember the “de.” Is that mandatory?

For 9), you’re running into the fact that mood choice interacts with meaning, not with tense alone. Here’s how to “know”: “Aunque” has two meanings: - Concession about a fact (“even though X is true”) → indicative. - Concession regardless of truth (“even if X happens/is true”) → subjunctive. Your sentence “Aunque llueva, salgo siempre” clearly fits the “regardless of whether it rains” meaning, so subjunctive is appropriate, and the habitual “salgo siempre” is fine. It’s like: “Rain or no rain, I always go out.” That’s coherent. If you want to disambiguate, you can add words: - Aunque llueva, salgo igual / de todos modos. (even if it rains, I go anyway) - Aunque llueve, salgo igual. (even though it’s raining, I go anyway) About “me alegro (de) que”: In standard grammar, “alegrarse de” takes “de”: - Me alegro de tu éxito. - Me alegro de que hayas venido. In some regions and informal speech, “me alegro que…” is heard, but in careful writing and most teaching contexts, include “de.” So: not absolutely “mandatory” in the sense that people will understand you without it, but it’s strongly recommended.
Can we do “esperar”? Because I think it means both “to hope” and “to wait,” and it changes the mood, right? I always mess it up.

Yes, “esperar” is a classic trap. 1) Esperar = to hope (wish) → subjunctive in the subordinate clause: - Espero que vengas. (I hope you come.) - Espero que todo salga bien. (I hope everything turns out well.) 2) Esperar = to wait for (no subordinate clause with “que” in the same way; usually direct object or “a que” in some cases): - Espero el autobús. (I’m waiting for the bus.) - Te espero. (I’m waiting for you.) You can also say: - Espero a que llegue el autobús. (I’m waiting for the bus to arrive.) Here, “a que” + subjunctive because it’s a time event not yet realized. Compare: - Espero que llega el autobús. sounds wrong for “I’m waiting for the bus to arrive.” It would be interpreted as “I hope the bus arrives” (and still should be subjunctive: Espero que llegue el autobús). So: - hope: “espero que” + subjunctive. - wait: “esperar” + noun/pronoun; or “esperar a que” + subjunctive (waiting for an event).
That helps. Now “ojalá.” I know it uses subjunctive, but which tense? People say “Ojalá que llueva” and “Ojalá lloviera.” What’s the difference?

“Ojalá” expresses a wish. The tense of the subjunctive encodes how realistic or time-anchored the wish is. 1) Ojalá + present subjunctive: wish about present/future that seems possible: - Ojalá llueva mañana. (Hopefully it rains tomorrow.) - Ojalá venga. (I hope he comes.) 2) Ojalá + imperfect subjunctive: wish that’s more remote, less likely, or contrary to present reality: - Ojalá lloviera (pero no hay nubes). (I wish it would rain [but it doesn’t look like it].) - Ojalá tuviera más tiempo. (I wish I had more time.) This is typically contrary to current reality. 3) Ojalá + pluperfect subjunctive: wish about the past (regret): - Ojalá hubiera estudiado más. (I wish I had studied more.) You can think: - present subjunctive = “I hope” (possible). - imperfect subjunctive = “I wish” (less real / counterfactual). - pluperfect subjunctive = “I wish I had…” (past regret).
Good. Now explain “que” vs “de que.” I keep getting corrected for “pienso de que” and “creo de que.” But then “me alegro de que” does take “de.” How do I know?

This is about verb + preposition patterns, not mood. Some verbs take “de” before a noun or clause; others don’t. Using “de” incorrectly is called “dequeísmo” (adding “de” when it doesn’t belong). Omitting it when it does belong is “queísmo.” Common verbs that take NO “de” before “que”: - creer que, pensar que, decir que, saber que, afirmar que, negar que, esperar que (hope), imaginar que, suponer que Correct: - Creo que es tarde. (not “creo de que”) - Pienso que tienes razón. (not “pienso de que”) Verbs/expressions that DO take “de” before “que”: - alegrarse de que - acordarse de que - darse cuenta de que - olvidarse de que - arrepentirse de que - estar seguro de que - tener miedo de que - quejarse de que (often “de que”) - tratar de que (less common; more often “tratar de” + infinitive) A test: replace the “que clause” with “eso” (that). If the verb takes “de eso,” it likely takes “de que”: - Me alegro de eso → Me alegro de que… - Me doy cuenta de eso → Me doy cuenta de que… But: - Creo eso (no “de”) → Creo que… It’s not perfect, but it’s a good learner test.
I also see “insistir en que.” That’s not “de,” it’s “en.” And it uses subjunctive, right?

Right on both counts. - insistir en que + subjunctive (when it’s insisting that someone do something / recommendation): - Insisto en que vengas. (I insist that you come.) But if “insistir” is followed by a statement you’re asserting as true, you can sometimes see indicative, though subjunctive is very common because insisting often implies influence/pressure: - Insistió en que tenía razón. (He insisted that he was right.) Here it’s more like reporting his claim; many speakers use indicative because it’s “his asserted fact,” though subjunctive also appears: insistió en que tuviera razón (less common, can sound like the speaker doubts it or frames it differently). For learners, the safe baseline: - insistir en que + subjunctive (especially for actions/requests). And memorize the preposition: insistir en.
So even “insistió en que tenía razón” is possible. I’m back to screaming.

The variation happens most with “verbs of communication/attitude” (decir, insistir, afirmar) when the subordinate clause is treated as reported content vs influenced action. If you keep it simple: - If it’s a request/command/influence: subjunctive. - Insistió en que lo hiciéramos. (He insisted that we do it.) - If it’s reporting someone’s assertion: indicative is common. - Insistió en que tenía razón. (He insisted that he was right.) This is similar to English: “He insisted that he was right” vs “He insisted that we leave.” Spanish just has mood marking to reflect the difference.
Can we talk about “lo que” and “el hecho de que”? I saw “El hecho de que…” and it used subjunctive and I thought “hecho” means fact. So why subjunctive?

Excellent question. “El hecho de que…” can take either mood depending on how it’s used. 1) If “el hecho de que” is used to introduce a real, asserted fact, indicative is natural: - El hecho de que no vino es preocupante. (The fact that he didn’t come is worrying.) Here, not coming is treated as a fact. 2) Subjunctive can appear when “el hecho de que” is used more like “the mere fact that / the circumstance that,” often with an evaluative main clause, or when the speaker isn’t presenting it as an established fact (or is framing it as a consideration): - El hecho de que no venga hoy me preocupa. (The fact that he might not come today worries me.) Here it’s not confirmed; it’s a possibility. - El hecho de que sea difícil no significa que sea imposible. (The fact that it is difficult doesn’t mean it’s impossible.) Some speakers still use subjunctive here as a stylistic/structural tendency because “el hecho de que” behaves like a nominalizer and often pairs with evaluative/argumentative contexts. Prescriptively, many grammar guides recommend indicative if it’s truly a fact. But in real usage, subjunctive is common after “el hecho de que,” especially in formal writing. So: it’s a notorious “subjunctive magnet.” For “lo que”: that’s not mood-related; it’s a relative pronoun (“what/that which”): - No entiendo lo que dices. (indicative because “decir” is a statement) - Me alegra lo que dices. (still indicative; the embedded clause is a relative clause, not a “que” noun clause) But you can have subjunctive in a relative clause with “lo que” if it’s hypothetical/unknown, though it’s less common: - Haré lo que pueda. (I’ll do what I can.) → subjunctive because it’s about an open-ended limit.
“Haré lo que pueda” makes sense. So relative clauses can also be subjunctive if it’s not a specific known thing. Like “el libro que sea fácil.” Okay.

Exactly. Relative clause subjunctive = non-specific/unknown/desired antecedent. More examples: - Necesito alguien que me ayude. (I need someone to help me.) Someone unspecified. - Tengo un amigo que me ayuda. (I have a friend who helps me.) Specific friend. - Buscamos un lugar donde podamos hablar. (a place where we can talk—any suitable place) - Estamos en el lugar donde podemos hablar. (this specific place)
Wait “donde podamos” vs “donde podemos.” That’s another thing—subjunctive after “donde” too?

Yes. “Donde,” “cuando,” “como” can act as relative adverbs introducing relative clauses, and they follow the same specificity rule. - Conozco un lugar donde puedes estudiar. (I know a place where you can study.) If you mean a specific place you know, indicative is natural. - Busco un lugar donde puedas estudiar. (I’m looking for a place where you can study.) Not identified → subjunctive. Similarly: - Hay días cuando me siento bien. (There are days when I feel good.) (indicative; describing real days) - Busco un día cuando puedas venir. (I’m looking for a day when you can come.) (subjunctive; not fixed) And “como”: - Lo hago como me dijiste. (the way you told me) indicative because it’s a known instruction - Lo haré como puedas/como quieras. (however you can/want) subjunctive because it’s open-ended.
I think I’m starting to see patterns, but then I open a book and see “No creo que es…” somewhere and I panic. Is that just wrong?

Usually, “No creo que…” takes subjunctive: “No creo que sea/que venga.” If you see “No creo que es” it could be: 1) A mistake (common in learner texts or informal writing). 2) A dialectal/colloquial usage where indicative appears, especially if the speaker is actually asserting something strongly and “no creo” is used rhetorically (like “I don’t believe it—(but actually) it is…”). But that’s not the standard teaching norm. For benchmarking your own production, stick to: - no creo que + subjunctive - no pienso que + subjunctive - no me parece que + subjunctive And: - creo que + indicative - pienso que + indicative - me parece que + indicative
“Me parece que” is indicative even though it’s like “it seems to me,” which is uncertain. Why isn’t it subjunctive?

Because “me parece que” typically functions as an assertion of your assessment. You’re still committing to the proposition as your conclusion. - Me parece que tienes razón. (I think you’re right.) Negated: - No me parece que tengas razón. (I don’t think you’re right.) → subjunctive. So it patterns like creer/pensar. However, there are other “seems” expressions: - Parece que + indicative often (Parece que está lloviendo.) - Es posible que + subjunctive (less commitment) - Puede que + subjunctive (Puede que tenga razón.) “Puede que” is a good one: it’s almost always subjunctive.
“Puede que” always subjunctive. Okay, that’s at least one stable thing. Now can we drill connectors: “a menos que,” “con tal de que,” “sin que,” “en caso de que.” Are these always subjunctive?

These are largely “subjunctive-by-default” because they express conditions, exceptions, or requirements—non-asserted contexts. - a menos que + subjunctive (unless): - No salgo a menos que llueva. (I won’t go out unless it rains.) (Usually it’s “a menos que llueva,” yes.) - con tal de que + subjunctive (provided that): - Te ayudo con tal de que me lo pidas. - sin que + subjunctive (without [someone] doing): - Se fue sin que lo viéramos. (He left without us seeing him.) - en caso de que + subjunctive (in case): - En caso de que haya problemas, llámame. Also: - siempre que + subjunctive when it means “as long as/provided that”: - Puedes venir siempre que me avises. But “siempre que” can be indicative when it means “whenever” (habitual): - Siempre que viene, trae algo. (Whenever he comes, he brings something.) So most of these are reliably subjunctive, with “siempre que” being a notable two-meaning connector.
That “siempre que” split is evil. Can you give more examples to separate them?

Sure. 1) “Siempre que” = “provided that / as long as” → subjunctive: - Te lo presto siempre que me lo devuelvas mañana. (I’ll lend it to you as long as you return it tomorrow.) This sets a condition; returning is not asserted. 2) “Siempre que” = “whenever / every time that” → indicative: - Siempre que me llama, estoy ocupado. (Whenever he calls me, I’m busy.) Habitual pattern. - Siempre que llueve, se inunda la calle. (Whenever it rains, the street floods.) General truth. If you can replace it with “con tal de que / a condición de que,” it’s the conditional meaning → subjunctive. If you can replace it with “cada vez que,” it’s habitual → indicative.
Good trick. Now “hasta que.” I see “hasta que llegue” vs “hasta que llegó.” Similar to “cuando,” right?

Exactly. “Hasta que” works like “cuando” in terms of completed vs pending event. - No salgas hasta que termine. (Don’t leave until I finish.) Finish is pending → subjunctive. - No salí hasta que terminó. (I didn’t leave until he finished.) Completed past → indicative. - Espero aquí hasta que llegas. (I wait here until you arrive [habitually]) → indicative habitual. - Esperaré aquí hasta que llegues. (I will wait here until you arrive [this time]) → subjunctive.
Okay. Now I need help with sequence of tenses. If the main verb is past, do I have to use imperfect subjunctive? Like “Quería que vinieras.” But can I say “Quería que vengas”? I hear both.

Standard sequence of tenses: - Main clause present/future/command → present subjunctive in subordinate clause: - Quiero que vengas. - Te pediré que vengas. - Pídeme que vengas. - Main clause past (preterite/imperfect/conditional) → imperfect subjunctive: - Quería que vinieras. - Te pedí que vinieras. - Me gustaría que vinieras. - Main clause present perfect sometimes patterns with present: - He querido que vengas / He querido que vinieras (both possible depending on whether the wanting is still relevant now). Now, “Quería que vengas” does occur in some varieties (notably in parts of Latin America) where the sequence of tenses is looser and present subjunctive can be used if the subordinate action is present/future relative to now. But in many teaching norms and in much of Spain, “quería que vinieras” is preferred. So for “safe” standard usage: - Quería que vinieras (even if the coming is still in the future relative to that past wanting).

So basically: follow the textbook sequence unless I’m intentionally copying a dialect. Fine. Another: present perfect subjunctive vs imperfect subjunctive. “Me alegro de que hayas venido” vs “me alegraba de que vinieras.” How do I pick?

Use the subjunctive tense that matches the time relationship of the subordinate action to the main clause reference time. Present perfect subjunctive (haya(s) + participle) is used when the subordinate action is completed before the present reference or is relevant to the present: - Me alegro de que hayas venido. (I’m glad you came [and it matters now].) - Es una pena que no hayas podido venir. Imperfect subjunctive is used when the main clause is in the past and the subordinate action is simultaneous or future relative to that past reference: - Me alegraba de que vinieras. (I was glad that you were coming / came [in that past context].) - Quería que vinieras. (I wanted you to come [then].) Pluperfect subjunctive (hubiera/hubiese + participle) is used for actions completed before a past reference: - Me alegraba de que hubieras venido. (I was glad that you had come [before that moment].) So it’s basically a relative time system: present vs past reference + whether the subordinate event is prior or not.

Can we do a focused drill on “por” vs “para” with subjunctive? Like “para que” is subjunctive, but “porque” sometimes triggers subjunctive too? I’ve seen “porque” with subjunctive and I thought it was always indicative.

Good catch: “para que” is purpose → subjunctive. “Porque” is usually cause/reason → indicative, but it can take subjunctive in specific pragmatic contexts, especially when the reason is denied, questioned, or not asserted. 1) Purpose: para que + subjunctive - Te lo digo para que lo sepas. (so that you know) 2) Cause asserted: porque + indicative - No fui porque estaba cansado. (I didn’t go because I was tired.) You assert the tiredness as the reason. 3) “Not because…” (denying that cause): no… porque + subjunctive often, especially when followed by “sino porque…” - No lo hice porque quisiera ayudarte, sino porque era necesario. (I didn’t do it because I wanted to help you, but because it was necessary.) Here, “quisiera” is subjunctive because that motive is explicitly rejected. 4) Questioning a reason can also lead to subjunctive: - No es porque sea difícil… (It’s not because it’s difficult…) This is a set structure where the cause is being framed, often with subjunctive. But as a learner baseline: - porque → indicative - para que → subjunctive And remember the special “no… porque + subj” when the cause is negated.
That “No lo hice porque quisiera…” example is actually useful. So the subjunctive is tied to denying the reason, not the verb “porque” itself.
Exactly. “Porque” doesn’t “trigger” subjunctive by itself; the discourse context does (negation, denial of causality, etc.).
Another thing: “The best way to…” sentences. “Busco la mejor manera de que…” vs “para que…” vs infinitive. I always get tangled.

Typically: - “La mejor manera de + infinitive” when the subject is the same or the clause can be expressed as an infinitive: - La mejor manera de aprender es practicar. - If you need a subordinate clause with a different subject or a purpose clause: - La mejor manera de que ellos aprendan… is possible but often sounds heavy; many prefer rephrasing. Better: - La mejor manera de lograr que ellos aprendan es… (lograr que + subjunctive) - La mejor manera para que ellos aprendan es… (para que + subjunctive), though “manera para que” can sound less idiomatic than “manera de” + infinitive or “forma de” + infinitive. Common idiomatic patterns: - La mejor manera de + infinitive - La mejor forma de + infinitive - La mejor manera de hacer que + subjunctive (hacer que = to cause) - La mejor manera de lograr que + subjunctive Examples: - La mejor manera de hacer que estudien es quitar distracciones. - La mejor forma de lograr que entiendan es explicar con ejemplos.
So “hacer que” and “lograr que” are like influence verbs, so subjunctive. Makes sense.
Exactly.
Can we go over “para que” vs “para” + infinitive again with subject change? I still screw it up.

Sure, with clean pairs: Same subject → para + infinitive: - Estudio para aprender. (I study in order to learn.) - Trabajo para ganar dinero. (I work to earn money.) Different subject → para que + subjunctive: - Estudio para que mis padres estén orgullosos. (I study so that my parents are proud.) - Te lo explico para que lo entiendas. (I explain it so that you understand it.) If you try to force different subjects with infinitive, it becomes ungrammatical or changes meaning: - Estudio para mis padres estar orgullosos ✗ So you need “para que estén.”
Okay. Now I want a list of the most common “subjunctive triggers” grouped, because otherwise I forget them.

Grouped high-frequency triggers: 1) Will / influence (requests, commands, recommendations): - querer que, pedir que, mandar que, exigir que, recomendar que, sugerir que, aconsejar que, insistir en que, permitir que, prohibir que, dejar que, hacer que, lograr que 2) Emotion / reaction: - alegrarse de que, sentir que (to regret), temer que, tener miedo de que, molestar que, sorprender que, gustar que (less common than “me gusta” + noun), odiar que, encantar que, es una lástima que 3) Doubt / denial / uncertainty: - dudar que, no creer que, no pensar que, no estar seguro de que, negar que, es posible que, es probable que, puede que, quizá(s) (often), tal vez (often) 4) Impersonal expressions / necessity / value judgments: - es necesario que, es importante que, es mejor que, es urgente que, conviene que, basta con que, es una pena que, es raro que, es lógico que (can vary), es posible que 5) Conjunctions that (often) require subjunctive: - para que, a fin de que - antes de que - sin que - a menos que - con tal de que - en caso de que - siempre que (when “as long as”) - hasta que (future/pending) - cuando (future) - aunque (hypothetical) - como (when meaning “provided that”: Como no vengas… = If you don’t come…) And always remember: triggers don’t override meaning in relative clauses; that’s a separate category.
“Como no vengas…” Wait, “como” can mean “if”? I thought it was “as/like.”

Yes. “Como” has a conditional threat/warning use meaning “if” (often with an implied consequence), and it uses subjunctive: - Como no vengas ahora, me voy. (If you don’t come now, I’m leaving.) - Como me digas eso otra vez, me enfado. (If you say that to me again, I’ll get angry.) This “como + subjunctive” is common in spoken Spanish and tends to be emphatic.
That’s new to me. Okay. Another: “whether” clauses. “No sé si…” is indicative, right? But “No sé que…” doesn’t exist. And “No sé si venga” sounds wrong to me.

Correct: “No sé si…” typically takes indicative because it’s an indirect yes/no question, not a subjunctive trigger. - No sé si viene. (I don’t know whether he’s coming.) - No sé si vino. (I don’t know whether he came.) - No sé si vendrá. (I don’t know whether he will come.) In some contexts, you might hear subjunctive after “no sé si” in certain dialects, but standard usage is indicative. If you want subjunctive with “whether,” you usually need a different structure, like: - No sé si es verdad que… (still indicative in the “si” clause) Or: - Puede que venga. (maybe he’ll come) (subjunctive, but not “si”) So your intuition is good: “No sé si venga” is generally nonstandard.
Good. At least that’s stable. Now: “I’m looking forward to…” How do you do that in Spanish with subjunctive? I always want to say “Espero por” or something and it comes out wrong.

Common ways: - Tengo ganas de + infinitive: Tengo ganas de verte. (I’m looking forward to seeing you.) - Estoy deseando + infinitive: Estoy deseando verte. (I’m really looking forward to seeing you.) - Espero + infinitive / Espero poder + infinitive (I hope to be able to…): Espero verte pronto. (I hope to see you soon.) If there’s a subject change: - Tengo ganas de que vengas. (I’m looking forward to you coming.) → subjunctive. - Estoy deseando que vengas. → subjunctive. “Esperar por” is used in some places meaning “to wait for,” but it’s regional; “esperar a” or just “esperar” is more standard: - Te espero. / Espero a Juan.
Okay. I need more practice with relative clauses because I keep defaulting to indicative. Can you give me like 20 sentences where I choose subjunctive or indicative?

Choose mood in the relative clause (indicative vs subjunctive) and explain briefly (specific vs non-specific). Fill the verb in parentheses. 1) Necesito un profesor que (explicar) bien la gramática. 2) Conozco a un profesor que (explicar) muy bien la gramática. 3) Buscamos un apartamento que (tener) mucha luz. 4) Vivimos en un apartamento que (tener) mucha luz. 5) Quiero un libro que me (ayudar) con el subjuntivo. 6) Tengo un libro que me (ayudar) con el subjuntivo. 7) No hay nadie que me (entender). 8) Hay alguien que me (entender). 9) ¿Tienes un bolígrafo que (funcionar)? 10) Tengo el bolígrafo que (funcionar) mejor. 11) Busco algo que (ser) más simple. 12) Compré algo que (ser) más simple. 13) Necesitamos un lugar donde (poder) estudiar en silencio. 14) Estamos en el lugar donde (poder) estudiar en silencio. 15) No encuentro nada que (servir). 16) Encontré algo que (servir) para esto. 17) Quiero hablar con alguien que (saber) la respuesta. 18) Hablé con alguien que (saber) la respuesta. 19) Busco una explicación que (tener) ejemplos claros. 20) Tengo una explicación que (tener) ejemplos claros.

Okay, I’ll try. I’m sure I’ll mess up. 1) explique (subj) 2) explica (ind) 3) tenga (subj) 4) tiene (ind) 5) ayude (subj) 6) ayuda (ind) 7) entienda (subj) 8) entiende (ind) 9) funcione (subj?) because it’s like any pen? 10) funciona (ind) 11) sea (subj) 12) es (ind) 13) podamos (subj) 14) podemos (ind) 15) sirva (subj) 16) sirve (ind) 17) sepa (subj) 18) sabía (ind?) because past 19) tenga (subj) 20) tiene (ind)

That’s excellent—almost all correct, and your reasoning is on track. Notes: 9) ¿Tienes un bolígrafo que funcione? → Yes, subjunctive is very common because you’re asking if such a pen exists/you have one that works (non-specific). Indicative can appear if you’re pointing at one and asking about it: ¿Tienes el bolígrafo que funciona? (specific). 18) Hablé con alguien que sabía la respuesta. → Indicative is right because it’s a specific person you actually spoke with, and you’re presenting “knew the answer” as factual description. You could also say “que supiera la respuesta” in some contexts, but it often shifts nuance (like “someone who would know,” or distancing/uncertainty about whether they truly knew). For straightforward narration, indicative is best. Everything else: good.
That nuance again. “Que supiera” sounds like I’m not sure they knew. So subjunctive can add distance even if the person existed?

Exactly. Subjunctive in relative clauses can sometimes signal not only non-specificity, but also that the property is not fully asserted by the speaker (uncertainty, evaluation, or “the kind of person who would…”). But the core learner rule remains: known/specific/asserted → indicative; sought/unknown/negated → subjunctive.
Can we do negation words like “nadie,” “nada,” “ninguno”? Those seem to force subjunctive sometimes.

Yes—negatives often create “nonexistence” contexts, which favor subjunctive in relative clauses. - No hay nadie que lo sepa. (There isn’t anyone who knows it.) → subjunctive (no such person exists in the speaker’s view) - No tengo nada que decir. (I have nothing to say.) Here it’s infinitive because same subject. - No veo nada que sea útil. (I don’t see anything that is useful.) → subjunctive, because “anything useful” is not identified/it’s like “nothing that qualifies.” But if you assert existence: - Hay alguien que lo sabe. (There’s someone who knows.) → indicative. Also with questions: - ¿Hay alguien que lo sepa? often subjunctive because existence is uncertain.

Now I want to tackle “subjunctive with future reference” but not in time clauses. Like “I’ll do it when…” we did. But what about “I hope that…” It’s still present subjunctive, not future subjunctive. Does Spanish even have future subjunctive?

Spanish does have a “future subjunctive” (e.g., “viniere,” “hubiere”), but it’s archaic and basically not used in modern everyday Spanish, except in some legal language and fixed expressions. Modern Spanish uses present subjunctive for future reference after subjunctive triggers: - Espero que vengas mañana. (present subjunctive, future meaning) - Cuando llegues, hablamos. (present subjunctive, future time clause) - Te llamaré para que me digas… (present subjunctive) So don’t worry about future subjunctive unless you’re reading old texts or legal documents.
Thank god. Now, can you explain the difference between “Quiero que lo hagas” and “Quiero hacerlo”? Like mood vs infinitive again, but also meaning. Sometimes “quiero que” feels too controlling.

Good observation: both are grammatical but differ in subject and sometimes pragmatic force. - Quiero hacerlo. = I want to do it. Same subject; more direct about your own action. - Quiero que lo hagas. = I want you to do it. Different subject; can sound like a request/order depending on context. To soften “quiero que lo hagas,” Spanish often uses: - ¿Puedes hacerlo? (Can you do it?) - Me gustaría que lo hicieras. (I’d like you to do it.) → imperfect subjunctive; more polite. - ¿Te importaría hacerlo? (Would you mind doing it?) - Sería bueno que lo hicieras. (It would be good if you did it.) So yes: “quiero que…” is grammatically fine but can sound demanding.
Great. More politeness layers. Now: “I suggest that…” That’s subjunctive, right? “Sugiero que…” But I also see “sugiero” with infinitive.

Both exist. - Sugiero que + subjunctive (especially with different subject): - Sugiero que estudies más. - Sugiero que revisemos el texto. (same subject possible too) - Sugerir + infinitive is also common, especially in instructions: - Sugiero estudiar más. - Sugiero revisar el texto. In practice: - “Sugiero + infinitive” can sound a bit like general advice. - “Sugiero que + subjunctive” can feel more direct or specific, and it’s necessary when you need an explicit subject different from the speaker.
Can you explain “the subjunctive in noun clauses” without the trigger list? Like the underlying logic? Because lists don’t stick.

Underlying logic: Spanish uses subjunctive in subordinate noun clauses when the main clause establishes a “non-assertive operator” over the proposition—meaning the speaker is not straightforwardly asserting the subordinate clause as true in the discourse. Instead, the main clause frames it as desired, required, doubted, denied, evaluated, or emotionally reacted to. You can categorize these operators: 1) Volitional / directive operator: wants the world to change - Quiero que P. - Pido que P. - Es necesario que P. Here P is not asserted; it’s a target or requirement. 2) Epistemic non-commitment operator: speaker withholds belief - Dudo que P. - No creo que P. P is not presented as true. 3) Affective/evaluative operator: speaker expresses attitude toward P rather than asserting P - Me alegra que P. - Es triste que P. Even if P is true, the communicative focus is the attitude. Contrast with assertive operators: - Sé que P. - Veo que P. - Es verdad que P. These assert P as true, so indicative. This “operator” framing is more abstract but more consistent than memorizing “WEDO” lists. The list is just a way to recognize operators quickly.
That’s the first explanation that feels like it’s not insulting my intelligence. Okay. Can we do “subjunctive after ‘que’ without any trigger”? Like “El que…” or “Que…” initial clauses?

There are a few “que” structures not based on the typical trigger list: 1) Jussive “que + subjunctive” commands (we covered): - ¡Que se vaya! 2) Concessive/idiomatic initial “Que…” meaning “even if/although” in some colloquial uses: - Que digan lo que quieran. (Let them say what they want / Even if they say whatever...) This is more like a permissive/jussive nuance. 3) “El que…” as a relative pronoun (“the one who…”). Mood depends on specificity/meaning: - El que estudia, aprende. (indicative: general truth, “he who studies learns”) - El que estudie, aprenderá. (subjunctive: more like “whoever studies will learn” / a conditional flavor) This is a neat pattern: subjunctive can give “whoever/anyone who” conditional meaning. Similarly: - Quien lo sabe, lo dice. (The person who knows it says it.) (indicative) - Quien lo sepa, que lo diga. (Whoever knows it, should say it.) (subjunctive + jussive)
That “El que estudie” vs “El que estudia” is interesting. So subjunctive can make it sound like a rule/condition rather than a description.
Exactly. Subjunctive often introduces a “set of possible cases” rather than describing an established set.
I think I need more of those minimal pairs because they make it click. Can you give me a bunch across categories?

Sure—here are compact minimal pairs with a one-line meaning difference: 1) Creo que viene. (I believe he’s coming.) No creo que venga. (I don’t think he’s coming.) 2) Es cierto que tiene razón. (It’s certain he’s right.) Es posible que tenga razón. (It’s possible he’s right.) 3) Cuando llega, me llama. (Whenever he arrives, he calls.) Cuando llegue, me llamará. (When he arrives [later], he’ll call.) 4) Aunque está cansado, sigue. (Even though he’s tired [fact], he continues.) Aunque esté cansado, seguirá. (Even if he’s tired, he’ll continue.) 5) Busco a alguien que sabe francés. (I’m looking for someone (specific) who knows French.) Busco a alguien que sepa francés. (I’m looking for someone (anyone) who knows French.) 6) Hay un libro que explica eso. (There’s a book that explains that.) No hay ningún libro que explique eso. (There isn’t any book that explains that.) 7) Me alegra que estés aquí. (I’m glad you’re here.) Veo que estás aquí. (I see you’re here.) 8) Te lo digo porque es importante. (I’m telling you because it’s important.) No te lo digo porque sea importante. (I’m not telling you because it’s important [that’s not the reason].) 9) Si tengo tiempo, voy. (If I have time, I’ll go.) Si tuviera tiempo, iría. (If I had time, I would go.) 10) Haré lo que puedo. (I’ll do what I can [sounds like a claim about ability now].) Haré lo que pueda. (I’ll do whatever I can [open-ended, “as much as possible”].) If you want, you can paraphrase each in English to confirm you’re getting the nuance.
10) is confusing. “Haré lo que puedo” sounds okay to me.

It can sound okay, but it changes meaning. “Haré lo que puedo” is less idiomatic and tends to sound like a present-tense statement about current ability (“I do what I can”) rather than a commitment about the future with an open limit. More natural: - Hago lo que puedo. (I do what I can.) → indicative, habitual/present fact. - Haré lo que pueda. (I’ll do what I can / whatever I can.) → subjunctive because “what I can (manage)” is not a fixed known quantity; it’s an open-ended maximum. You might also see: - Haré lo que pueda para ayudarte. (very common) So your intuition that it’s “okay” is understandable, but native usage strongly prefers the subjunctive in the future-oriented version.

Strong performance. Corrections and notes: All correct except one key item: 20) Cuando (terminar), salimos. (habitual) → indicative: cuando termino, salimos. You got it. Everything else is good, with two “variation” points: 28) ¿Crees que (poder) hacerlo? - You chose “puedas.” That’s acceptable in many places because it’s a non-assertive question about ability/likelihood. - “¿Crees que puedes hacerlo?” is also very common (and maybe more common overall). Diagnostic: You’re not missing much. The main pattern you should keep reinforcing is: - Time clauses: habitual/past = indicative; future/pending = subjunctive. - “Aunque”: fact = indicative; hypothetical = subjunctive. You already applied both correctly. Your real risk zone seems to be “variation contexts” (questions with creer, no dudar que, el hecho de que, etc.). For those, pick one consistent standard and stick to it.
Variation contexts are the worst because I want one right answer. But okay. Can you give me a “standard Spanish” preference list for those variation contexts so I stop spiraling?

Yes—here are conservative, widely accepted preferences (good for formal writing and most classroom norms): 1) ¿Crees que…? → indicative preferred: - ¿Crees que viene? (Subjunctive exists, but stick to indicative to avoid second-guessing.) 2) No dudo que… → indicative preferred: - No dudo que es verdad. 3) Después de que… → indicative preferred (when factual): - Después de que llegó, hablamos. (Some places accept subjunctive; choose indicative.) 4) El hecho de que… → indicative preferred when it’s truly a fact: - El hecho de que no vino es importante. Use subjunctive only if it’s not confirmed or is framed as hypothetical: - El hecho de que no venga me preocupa. (not coming is possible/future) 5) Quizás / tal vez → either; pick one strategy: - Use subjunctive when you mean “maybe” (more cautious): Quizás venga. - Use indicative when you’re fairly sure: Quizás viene. If you don’t want nuance, default to subjunctive. 6) Puede que → subjunctive almost always: - Puede que venga. 7) Aunque… → decide based on “even though” vs “even if.” If uncertain, ask yourself: do I know it’s true? If yes → indicative. If no/doesn’t matter → subjunctive. This gives you a stable “house style.”

Good. Now I want to understand why Spanish uses subjunctive after “buscar” sometimes. Like “busco un libro que…” We did. But I’ve also seen “Busco el libro que…” which is indicative. Is it just “un vs el”?

“Un vs el” is a strong clue, but the real factor is specificity/identifiability. - Busco un libro que explique el subjuntivo. → any book that explains it (subjunctive). - Busco el libro que explica el subjuntivo. → a specific book known to exist (indicative). But you can mix articles depending on meaning: - Busco un libro que explica el subjuntivo. This can be used if you have a particular book in mind but you’re describing it as “a book that explains…” (indefinite article used descriptively). Still, many speakers would prefer indicative here because you’re implying it exists and is identifiable to you. And you can also do: - Busco el libro que explique el subjuntivo. This is less common but could appear if you’re not sure which book qualifies as “the one that would explain it best” (selection among possibilities) → subjunctive because the property is used as a criterion rather than asserted fact. So: article helps, but meaning is decisive.
So basically, mood reflects whether the clause is descriptive (indicative) or selecting/defining a desired item (subjunctive). That actually makes sense.
Exactly. Relative clause subjunctive often has a “selection” feel.
I’m going to ask a very specific thing I keep messing up: “I’m happy you came” vs “I’m happy that you’re coming.” Is it “Me alegra que hayas venido” vs “Me alegra que vengas”? And if it’s future?

Yes: - Me alegra que hayas venido. = I’m glad you came (completed action before now). - Me alegra que vengas. = I’m glad you’re coming / that you come (could be present habitual or near-future depending on context). For explicit future, you can still use present subjunctive: - Me alegra que vengas mañana. (I’m glad you’re coming tomorrow.) Or: - Me alegra que vayas a venir mañana. (more explicit “going to come”) If the main clause is past: - Me alegró que vinieras. (I was glad you came/were coming.) - Me alegró que hubieras venido. (I was glad you had come.)
Great. Now, last thing: sometimes I see “sea” in places where I would use “es,” like “Sea como sea.” That’s subjunctive but it’s not triggered by anything. Why?

That’s a fixed concessive idiom meaning “be that as it may / whatever the case may be.” It uses subjunctive to express “regardless of what it is,” i.e., non-specific/indifferent-to-truth. Common idioms with subjunctive: - Sea como sea… (Whatever happens / Anyway…) - Cueste lo que cueste… (No matter what it costs…) - Pase lo que pase… (Whatever happens…) - Venga quien venga… (Whoever comes…) These are like “free-choice” constructions (no matter who/what/when), and subjunctive fits because it’s not asserting a particular case.
Okay, that’s actually a satisfying explanation. I still hate the subjunctive, but at least it’s less random. Can you summarize the whole thing in one page worth of text I can reread when I’m angry?

Use subjunctive when the subordinate clause is not being asserted as a straightforward fact, but is framed as (1) desired/required, (2) doubted/denied/uncertain, (3) reacted to emotionally/evaluated, or (4) pending/hypothetical/non-specific. In “que” noun clauses, memorize the operator contrast: assertive main clauses (sé que, veo que, es verdad que, creo que) → indicative; non-assertive operators (quiero que, es necesario que, me alegra que, dudo que, no creo que, es posible que, puede que) → subjunctive. In time clauses (cuando, en cuanto, hasta que), use subjunctive for future/pending events (Te llamaré cuando llegue) and indicative for habitual/past (Te llamo cuando llego; Te llamé cuando llegué). With “aunque,” indicative = “even though” (known fact: Aunque llueve…) and subjunctive = “even if” (hypothetical: Aunque llueva…). In relative clauses, indicative describes a known/specific antecedent (Tengo un libro que explica…) while subjunctive selects an unknown/nonexistent/desired antecedent (Busco un libro que explique…; No hay nadie que lo sepa). Purpose clauses with “para que” take subjunctive; same-subject purpose uses infinitive (Estudio para aprender / Estudio para que entiendas). Conditionals: real “si” uses indicative (Si tengo tiempo, voy); hypothetical uses imperfect subjunctive + conditional (Si tuviera tiempo, iría); past counterfactual uses pluperfect subjunctive + conditional perfect (Si hubiera tenido…, habría…). When there’s variation (¿Crees que…?, no dudo que…, después de que…), pick a consistent standard: default to indicative with “¿Crees que…?” and “no dudo que…,” and indicative after “después de que” for factual events. Keep drilling minimal pairs until the meaning difference (assertion vs non-assertion; specific vs non-specific; factual vs hypothetical; completed vs pending) feels automatic.
